reasoning: Each thread does only 1 global load and 1 global store for ~8 bytes of DRAM traffic, while the kernel body is a long chain of dependent scalar operations including multiple divides, so arithmetic intensity is only on the order of ~3–4 FLOP/byte—far below the 62 FLOP/byte ridge point and not enough to make it compute-bound. Because memory traffic is minimal and perfectly coalesced, DRAM bandwidth utilization should stay low rather than >50% peak, so the dominant limit is long-scoreboard/dependency latency from the serialized math pipeline, not bandwidth.
raw_response:
BOTTLENECK: latency-bound
CONFIDENCE: HIGH
REASONING: Each thread does only 1 global load and 1 global store for ~8 bytes of DRAM traffic, while the kernel body is a long chain of dependent scalar operations including multiple divides, so arithmetic intensity is only on the order of ~3–4 FLOP/byte—far below the 62 FLOP/byte ridge point and not enough to make it compute-bound. Because memory traffic is minimal and perfectly coalesced, DRAM bandwidth utilization should stay low rather than >50% peak, so the dominant limit is long-scoreboard/dependency latency from the serialized math pipeline, not bandwidth.
